description:
You are the release-manager for three internal micro-services.  
    Each service publishes a small HTML page (already present on disk) that exposes its *latest* release tag and release date.

    Goals for this session
    ----------------------
    1. Read the three HTML pages that already exist under  
       /home/user/releases/  
           ├── auth-service.html  
           ├── payment-service.html  
           └── notification-service.html  
    2. Extract – strictly from the first <div class="latest-release"> block found in each file –  
       • the textual content of the <span class="version"> element (strip the leading “v”), and  
       • the textual content of the <span class="released"> element.  
    3. Create the directory  /home/user/deployment/  (if it does not yet exist).
    4. Generate a single YAML file named  /home/user/deployment/release_manifest.yaml  that **exactly** follows the format below (indentation and dash placement must match so automated checks can diff it byte-for-byte):

services:
  - name: auth-service
    version: 1.8.3
    released: 2023-07-18
  - name: payment-service
    version: 3.2.0
    released: 2023-07-20
  - name: notification-service
    version: 2.0.5
    released: 2023-07-21

       • keys are all lower-case.  
       • version strings must **not** include the leading “v”.  
       • maintain the shown ordering (auth → payment → notification).

    5. Create a plaintext log file  /home/user/deployment/scrape.log  capturing, line-by-line, the progress messages shown below.  
       The four lines must appear **in exactly this order**, verbatim:

[INFO] Starting scrape...
[INFO] Fetched auth-service: v1.8.3 (2023-07-18)
[INFO] Fetched payment-service: v3.2.0 (2023-07-20)
[INFO] Fetched notification-service: v2.0.5 (2023-07-21)
[INFO] Manifest written to /home/user/deployment/release_manifest.yaml

    6. You may implement the scrape any way you like (python with a headless browser, curl & grep, etc.),
       but the two output files named above MUST exist with the exact contents shown so that automated tests pass.

    Hints (not mandatory)
    • Using Python: `html.parser` + regex or BeautifulSoup works fine in a headless context.  
    • Remember to strip the leading “v” when you write the YAML.

    The entire task should require roughly 5-10 shell commands or fewer.
